title.zh: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳
title.en: Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu
title.raw: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳 Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu

sentences.zh: 季布，楚人也，為任俠有名。

translations.literal: Ji Bu was a man of Chu; as one who practiced chivalrous violence on others' behalf (任俠), he had a name.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was a native of Chu and was widely known as a knight-errant who took up others' causes.

sentences.zh: 項籍使將兵，數窘漢王。

translations.literal: Xiang Ji had him command troops; several times he put the King of Han in straits.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu placed him in command of troops, and again and again he drove the King of Han into desperate positions.

sentences.zh: 項籍滅，高祖購求布千金，敢有舍匿，罪三族。

translations.literal: When Xiang Ji was destroyed, the High Ancestor offered a thousand catties of gold to seek Ji Bu; whoever dared shelter or hide him, crime of the three clans.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, the High Ancestor offered a bounty of a thousand catties of gold for Ji Bu; anyone who dared harbor him would be punished with extermination of three generations of his kin.

sentences.zh: 布匿濮陽周氏，周氏曰：「漢求將軍急，跡且至臣家，能聽臣，臣敢進計；

translations.literal: Bu hid with the Zhou clan of Puyang. The Zhou clan said: "Han seeks the general urgently; tracks will soon reach my house. If you can heed your servant, your servant dares advance a plan;
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu took refuge with the Zhou family of Puyang. They told him, "The court is hunting you relentlessly; their trail will soon lead to my door. If you will trust me, I will put forward a plan;

sentences.zh: 即否，願先自剄。」

translations.literal: If not, I wish first to cut my own throat."
translations.idiomatic: If not, I would rather slit my own throat first."

sentences.zh: 布許之。

translations.literal: Bu assented to it.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu agreed.

sentences.zh: 乃髡鉗布，衣褐，置廣柳車中，并與其家僮數十人，之魯朱家所賣之。

translations.literal: Thereupon they shaved Bu's head and put the cangue on him, clothed him in coarse cloth, placed him in a broad-willow (coffin) cart, together with several tens of his household slaves, and went to the place of Zhu Jia of Lu to sell him.
translations.idiomatic: They shaved Ji Bu's head, clamped him in the iron collar, dressed him in a laborer's hemp, hid him in a hearse-like cart with several dozen household retainers, and took the party to Zhu Jia of Lu to be "sold" as slaves.

sentences.zh: 朱家心知其季布也，買置田舍。

translations.literal: Zhu Jia in his heart knew he was Ji Bu; he bought him and placed him in field lodgings.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jia recognized him as Ji Bu, bought him, and installed him on a farm.

sentences.zh: 乃之雒陽見汝陰侯滕公，說曰：「季布何罪？

translations.literal: Thereupon he went to Luoyang and saw the Marquis of Ru-yin, Lord Teng, and persuaded, saying: "What crime has Ji Bu?
translations.idiomatic: He then traveled to Luoyang to see the Marquis of Ru-yin, Lord Teng of Xia, and argued, "What wrong has Ji Bu done?

sentences.zh: 臣各為其主用，職耳。

translations.literal: Ministers each for their lord were employed; it was duty only.
translations.idiomatic: Each minister served his own lord—that was nothing but duty.

sentences.zh: 項氏臣豈可盡誅邪？

translations.literal: Xiang's clan's ministers—how could they all be executed?
translations.idiomatic: Surely you cannot put every minister who served the Xiangs to the sword?

sentences.zh: 今上始得天下，而以私怨求一人，何示不廣也！

translations.literal: Now the sovereign has just obtained the empire, yet by private resentment seeks one man—how this shows lack of breadth!
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven has only just won the realm; to hunt one man out of personal spite shows how narrow his magnanimity would appear.

sentences.zh: 且以季布之賢，漢求之急如此，此不北走胡，南走越耳。

translations.literal: Moreover, by Ji Bu's worthiness, Han seeking him with such urgency—this is nothing but north fleeing to the Hu, south fleeing to Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, a man of Ji Bu's caliber, pressed this hard, will flee north to the Xiongnu or south to Yue—there is nowhere else for him to go.

sentences.zh: 夫忌壯士以資敵國，此伍子胥所以鞭荊平之墓也。

translations.literal: As for envying a stalwart and thereby aiding an enemy state—this is why Wu Zixu whipped King Ping of Jing's tomb.
translations.idiomatic: To drive a brave man into the arms of a rival is the very thing that made Wu Zixu exhume King Ping of Chu and flog his corpse.

sentences.zh: 君何不從容為上言之？」

translations.literal: Why do you not at leisure for the sovereign speak it?"
translations.idiomatic: Why do you not find a suitable moment and put this to the emperor?"

sentences.zh: 滕公心知朱家大俠，意布匿其所，乃許諾。

translations.literal: Lord Teng in his heart knew Zhu Jia was a great knight-errant; he suspected Bu was hidden at his place; thereupon he promised.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Teng knew Zhu Jia for a famous knight-errant and guessed Ji Bu lay concealed with him; he agreed to speak.

sentences.zh: 侍間，果言如朱家指。

translations.literal: Waiting for an interval, he indeed spoke as Zhu Jia had indicated.
translations.idiomatic: When a chance came, he repeated Zhu Jia's argument almost word for word.

sentences.zh: 上乃赦布。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon pardoned Bu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then granted Ji Bu a pardon.

sentences.zh: 當是時，諸公皆多布能摧剛為柔，朱家亦以此名聞當世。

translations.literal: At this time, the various lords all praised Bu for being able to break hardness and make softness; Zhu Jia also by this had his name heard in that age.
translations.idiomatic: At the time, everyone admired Ji Bu for bending unyielding force into pliant survival, and Zhu Jia's fame spread through the realm for the same deed.

sentences.zh: 布召見，謝，拜郎中。

translations.literal: Bu was summoned and seen; he thanked; he was appointed gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was summoned to audience, expressed his gratitude, and was appointed a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 孝惠時，為中郎將。

translations.literal: In the time of Filial Hui, he was a leader of court gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Hui he served as a leader of the gentlemen-at-attendance of the palace.

sentences.zh: 單于嘗為書嫚呂太后，太后怒，召諸將議之。

translations.literal: The Chanyu once made a letter insulting Empress Dowager Lü; the empress dowager was angry and summoned the various generals to deliberate on it.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu had once sent a letter grossly insulting Empress Dowager Lü; enraged, she called her generals in council.

sentences.zh: 上將軍樊噲曰：「臣願得十萬眾，橫行匈奴中。」

translations.literal: Upper general Fan Kuai said: "Your servant wishes to obtain a hundred thousand masses and rampage through the middle of the Xiongnu."
translations.idiomatic: Fan Kuai, senior general, declared, "Give me a hundred thousand men and I will sweep through the heart of the Xiongnu steppe."

sentences.zh: 諸將皆阿呂太后，以噲言為然。

translations.literal: The various generals all fawned on Empress Dowager Lü and took Kuai's words as correct.
translations.idiomatic: The generals curried favor with the empress dowager and pronounced Fan Kuai right.

sentences.zh: 布曰：「樊噲可斬也。

translations.literal: Bu said: "Fan Kuai can be beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu said, "Fan Kuai deserves execution.

sentences.zh: 夫以高帝兵三十餘萬，困於平城，噲時亦在其中。

translations.literal: As for the High Ancestor's troops of more than three hundred thousand, trapped at Pingcheng—at that time Kuai also was within them.
translations.idiomatic: The High Ancestor took more than three hundred thousand men to Pingcheng and was surrounded there—and Fan Kuai was in that army.

sentences.zh: 今噲奈何以十萬眾橫行匈奴中，面謾！

translations.literal: Now how does Kuai dare, with a hundred thousand masses, rampage through the middle of the Xiongnu—lying to the face!
translations.idiomatic: How dare he now boast that a mere hundred thousand could ride roughshod over the Xiongnu—lying to our faces!

sentences.zh: 且秦以事胡，陳勝等起。

translations.literal: Moreover, Qin because of serving the Hu, Chen Sheng and others arose.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, the Qin dynasty provoked the northern tribes and Chen Sheng's rebellion followed.

sentences.zh: 今瘡痍未瘳，噲又面諛，欲搖動天下。」

translations.literal: Now wounds are not yet healed, and Kuai again to the face flatters, wishing to shake and move the empire."
translations.idiomatic: The wounds of war are still raw, and Fan Kuai flatters you to your face, ready to unsettle the whole realm."

sentences.zh: 是時殿上皆恐，太后罷朝，遂不復議擊匈奴事。

translations.literal: At this time on the hall all were fearful; the empress dowager dismissed court; thereupon they no longer deliberated on striking the Xiongnu matter.
translations.idiomatic: A chill fell over the hall; the empress dowager ended the session, and the court never again debated an invasion of the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 布為河東守。

translations.literal: Bu was Hedong governor.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was appointed governor of Hedong.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，人有言其賢，召欲以為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the time of Filial Wen, someone spoke of his worthiness; he was summoned with the intention of making him imperial clerk grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen, someone praised his abilities; the emperor summoned him intending to name him imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 人又言其勇，使酒難近。

translations.literal: Someone again spoke of his bravery, that he used wine and was hard to approach.
translations.idiomatic: Others countered that he was fierce, given to drink, and impossible to work with.

sentences.zh: 至，留邸一月，見罷。

translations.literal: When he arrived, he was detained in the hostel one month and was dismissed upon audience.
translations.idiomatic: When he reached the capital he was lodged a month, then dismissed from audience without appointment.

sentences.zh: 布進曰：「臣待罪河東，陛下無故召臣，此人必有以臣欺陛下者。

translations.literal: Bu advanced and said: "Your servant awaits punishment at Hedong; Your Majesty without reason summoned your servant—this person must have had something by which to deceive Your Majesty with your servant.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu stepped forward and said, "I held office at Hedong awaiting fault; Your Majesty summoned me without cause—someone must have oversold me to you.

sentences.zh: 今臣至，無所受事，罷去，此人必有毀臣者。

translations.literal: Now your servant has arrived, received no assignment, and was dismissed to depart—this person must have had one who slandered your servant.
translations.idiomatic: Now that I am here with no duties given and am sent away, someone must have slandered me.

sentences.zh: 夫陛下以一人譽召臣，一人毀去臣，臣恐天下有識者聞之，有以窺陛下。」

translations.literal: As for Your Majesty, by one man's praise summoning your servant, by one man's slander sending away your servant—your servant fears that those in the empire with understanding who hear it will have means to peer into Your Majesty."
translations.idiomatic: If a single voice of praise can summon me and a single voice of blame dismiss me, thoughtful men everywhere will draw their own conclusions about Your Majesty's judgment."

sentences.zh: 上默然，慚曰：「河東吾股肱郡，故特召君耳。」

translations.literal: The sovereign was silent, ashamed, and said: "Hedong is my thigh-arm county; therefore I specially summoned you, that is all."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor fell silent, then said with embarrassment, "Hedong is one of my vital commanderies; I called you chiefly for that reason."

sentences.zh: 布之官。

translations.literal: Bu went to his office.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu returned to his post.

sentences.zh: 辯士曹丘生數招權顧金錢，事貴人趙談等，與竇長君善。

translations.literal: Sophist Cao Qiu Sheng several times wielded power and looked to money, served the noble men Zhao Tan and others, and was good with Dou the elder lord.
translations.idiomatic: The rhetorician Cao Qiu Sheng traded on influence for cash, cultivated favorites such as Zhao Tan, and was close to Dou Zhang, the empress's brother.

sentences.zh: 布聞，寄書諫長君曰：「吾聞曹丘生非長者，勿與通。」

translations.literal: Bu heard; he sent a letter remonstrating the elder lord, saying: "I have heard Cao Qiu Sheng is not an elder; do not communicate with him."
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu sent Lord Dou a letter of warning: "Cao Qiu Sheng is no man of honor; keep your distance."

sentences.zh: 及曹丘生歸，欲得書請布。

translations.literal: When Cao Qiu Sheng returned, he wished to obtain a letter to request Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Qiu Sheng came home, he wanted a letter of introduction to Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 竇長君曰：「季將軍不說足下，足下無往。」

translations.literal: Dou the elder lord said: "General Ji is not pleased with you; you must not go."
translations.idiomatic: Lord Dou replied, "General Ji dislikes you; you had better not go."

sentences.zh: 固請書，遂行。

translations.literal: He firmly requested the letter; thereupon he went.
translations.idiomatic: Cao pressed until he got the letter and set out.

sentences.zh: 使人先發書，布果大怒，待曹丘。

translations.literal: He had someone first dispatch the letter; Bu indeed was greatly angry and awaited Cao Qiu.
translations.idiomatic: He sent the letter ahead; Ji Bu was furious and waited for Cao Qiu in a rage.

sentences.zh: 曹丘至，則揖布曰：「楚人諺曰『得黃金百，不如得季布諾』，足下何以得此聲梁楚之間哉？

translations.literal: When Cao Qiu arrived, then he bowed to Bu and said: "The people of Chu have a proverb: 'Obtaining a hundred weight of yellow gold is not as good as obtaining Ji Bu's promise'—how did you obtain this reputation between Liang and Chu?
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Qiu arrived, he bowed and said, "Chu has a saying: 'A hundred catties of gold are worth less than Ji Bu's word'—how did you earn such renown across Liang and Chu?

sentences.zh: 且僕與足下俱楚人，使僕游揚足下名於天下，顧不美乎？

translations.literal: Moreover, your servant and you are both men of Chu; if your servant were to roam and raise your name in the empire, would it not be beautiful?
translations.idiomatic: We are both men of Chu; let me spread your name through the empire—would that not serve you well?

sentences.zh: 何足下距僕之深也！」

translations.literal: Why do you reject your servant so deeply!"
translations.idiomatic: Why do you shut me out so completely!"

sentences.zh: 布乃大說。

translations.literal: Bu thereupon was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was won over at once.

sentences.zh: 引入，留數月，為上客，厚送之。

translations.literal: He led him in, detained several months, made him upper guest, and sent him off thickly.
translations.idiomatic: He brought him in, kept him for months as an honored guest, and sent him away with rich gifts.

sentences.zh: 布名所以益聞者，曹丘揚之也。

translations.literal: The reason Bu's name thereby increased in being heard was that Cao Qiu raised it.
translations.idiomatic: It was Cao Qiu Sheng's praise that made Ji Bu's name ring louder still.

sentences.zh: 布弟季心氣蓋關中，遇人恭謹，為任俠，方數千里，士爭為死。

translations.literal: Bu's younger brother Ji Xin, spirit covering Guanzhong, met people respectfully and carefully; as knight-errant, square several thousand li, knights vied to die for him.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu's younger brother Ji Xin dominated Guanzhong in sheer force of character; outwardly deferential, he acted the knight-errant over thousands of miles, and fighting men competed for the honor of dying in his service.

sentences.zh: 嘗殺人，亡吳，從爰絲匿，長事爰絲，弟畜灌夫、籍福之屬。

translations.literal: He once killed a man, fled to Wu, followed Yuan Si in hiding, served Yuan Si as elder, younger-brother-raised Guan Fu, Ji Fu's sort.
translations.idiomatic: After a killing he fled to Wu and hid with Yuan Ang, whom he served as an elder patron while treating Guan Fu, Ji Fu, and their like as junior companions.

sentences.zh: 嘗為中司馬，中尉郅都不敢加。

translations.literal: He once was central marshal; central commandant Zhi Du did not dare apply pressure.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to central marshal in the guard; even Zhi Du, the dreaded commandant of the capital, did not dare touch him.

sentences.zh: 少年多時時竊借其名以行。

translations.literal: Young men often secretly borrowed his name in order to act.
translations.idiomatic: Young blades constantly traded on his name behind his back.

sentences.zh: 當是時，季心以勇，布以諾，聞關中。

translations.literal: At this time, Ji Xin by bravery, Bu by promise, were heard of in Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: In those days Ji Xin was famed in Guanzhong for courage, Ji Bu for keeping his word.

sentences.zh: 布母弟丁公，為項羽將，逐窘高祖彭城西。

translations.literal: Bu's mother's younger brother Ding Gong, as Xiang Yu's general, pursued and put the High Ancestor in straits west of Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu's uncle Ding Gong, a general under Xiang Yu, harried the High Ancestor west of Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 短兵接，漢王急，顧謂丁公曰：「兩賢豈相厄哉！」

translations.literal: Short weapons joined; the King of Han was in urgency; turning he said to Ding Gong: "Two worthies—how could they mutually obstruct!"
translations.idiomatic: In the melee the King of Han was hard pressed; wheeling his horse he cried to Ding Gong, "Must two able men try to destroy each other?"

sentences.zh: 丁公引兵而還。

translations.literal: Ding Gong led troops and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Gong withdrew his troops.

sentences.zh: 及項王滅，丁公謁見高祖，以丁公徇軍中，曰：「丁公為項王臣不忠，使項王失天下者也。」

translations.literal: When the Hegemon-King was destroyed, Ding Gong went to audience the High Ancestor; using Ding Gong he displayed in the army, saying: "Ding Gong as Xiang Yu's minister was not loyal; he is the one who made the Hegemon-King lose the empire."
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, Ding Gong presented himself to the High Ancestor, who had him paraded before the host and declared, "Ding Gong served the Hegemon-King without loyalty; he is the man who cost Xiang Yu the empire."

sentences.zh: 遂斬之，曰：「使後為人臣無傚丁公也！」

translations.literal: Thereupon he beheaded him, saying: "Let those after who are ministers for others not imitate Ding Gong!"
translations.idiomatic: He had him executed, adding, "Let no minister ever take Ding Gong as his model!"

sentences.zh: 欒布，梁人也。

translations.literal: Luan Bu was a man of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu was a native of Liang.

sentences.zh: 彭越為家人時，嘗與布游，窮困，賣庸於齊，為酒家保。

translations.literal: When Peng Yue was a commoner, he once roamed with Bu; in poverty and distress, Bu sold labor in Qi and was a tavern guarantor.
translations.idiomatic: When Peng Yue was still a commoner, he and Luan Bu wandered together; penniless, Luan hired out his labor in Qi and stood surety for a wine merchant.

sentences.zh: 數歲別去，而布為人所略，賣為奴於燕。

translations.literal: Several years they parted and departed, and Bu was taken by someone in abduction and sold as a slave in Yan.
translations.idiomatic: After some years they parted, and Luan Bu was kidnapped and sold into slavery in Yan.

sentences.zh: 為其主家報仇，燕將臧荼舉以為都尉。

translations.literal: For his master's household he avenged a wrong; Yan general Zang Tu raised him and made him a commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He avenged his master's family; the Yan general Zang Tu, impressed, appointed him a commandant.

sentences.zh: 荼為燕王，布為將。

translations.literal: When Tu became Yan king, Bu was general.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu became King of Yan, Luan Bu served as his general.

sentences.zh: 及荼反，漢擊燕，虜布。

translations.literal: When Tu rebelled, Han struck Yan and captured Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu rebelled, Han attacked Yan and took Luan Bu prisoner.

sentences.zh: 梁王彭越聞之，乃言上，請贖布為梁大夫。

translations.literal: King of Liang Peng Yue heard it; thereupon he spoke to the sovereign and requested to ransom Bu to be Liang grandee.
translations.idiomatic: King Peng Yue of Liang petitioned the emperor to ransom him and appoint him a grandee of Liang.

sentences.zh: 使於齊，未反，漢召彭越責以謀反，夷三族，梟首雒陽，下詔有收視者輒捕之。

translations.literal: He was sent on mission to Qi; before he returned, Han summoned Peng Yue and charged him with plotting rebellion, exterminated three clans, displayed the head at Luoyang, issued an edict that whoever collected or viewed would be arrested.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu was on a mission to Qi when the court summoned Peng Yue, accused him of treason, wiped out three generations of his kin, and exposed his head in Luoyang; an edict threatened arrest for anyone who dared mourn him.

sentences.zh: 布還，奏事彭越頭下，祠而哭之。

translations.literal: Bu returned, reported affairs beneath Peng Yue's head, sacrificed and wept for him.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu returned, presented his report beneath Peng Yue's impaled head, and offered sacrifice with weeping.

sentences.zh: 吏捕以聞。

translations.literal: Officials arrested and reported.
translations.idiomatic: The authorities seized him and reported to the throne.

sentences.zh: 上召布罵曰：「若與彭越反邪？

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned Bu and reviled, saying: "You with Peng Yue rebelled?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had Luan Bu brought in and shouted, "Were you in league with Peng Yue?

sentences.zh: 吾禁人勿收，若獨祠而哭之，與反明矣。

translations.literal: I forbade people not to gather; you alone sacrificed and wept for him—with rebellion it is clear.
translations.idiomatic: I forbade anyone to bury him; you alone mourned him at sacrifice—your guilt is plain as rebellion.

sentences.zh: 趣亨之。」

translations.literal: Hurry boil him."
translations.idiomatic: Boil him at once."

sentences.zh: 方提趨湯，顧曰：「願一言而死。」

translations.literal: Just as he was being lifted and hurried toward the cauldron, turning he said: "I wish one word and then to die."
translations.idiomatic: As they dragged him toward the cauldron, he looked back and said, "Let me speak one sentence before I die."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「何言？」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: "What words?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked, "What is it?"

sentences.zh: 布曰：「方上之困彭城，敗滎陽、成皋間，項王所以不能遂西，徒以彭王居梁地，與漢合從苦楚也。

translations.literal: Bu said: "When Your Majesty was trapped at Pengcheng, defeated between Xingyang and Chenggao, the reason the Hegemon-King could not thereupon go west was solely because the King of Peng dwelt in Liang territory, with Han joining vertical alliance to distress Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu said, "When you were beleaguered at Pengcheng and driven back between Xingyang and Chenggao, the only reason Xiang Yu failed to push west was that the King of Peng held Liang and kept Han and Liang in alliance against Chu.

sentences.zh: 當是之時，彭王壹顧，與楚則漢破，與漢則楚破。

translations.literal: At this time, if the King of Peng had one look—if with Chu then Han was broken; if with Han then Chu was broken.
translations.idiomatic: At that moment a single shift of Peng Yue's weight—if he had thrown in with Chu, Han would have shattered; if with Han, Chu would have shattered.

sentences.zh: 且垓下之會，微彭王，項氏不亡。

translations.literal: Moreover, at the meeting of Gaixia, without the King of Peng, the Xiang clan would not have perished.
translations.idiomatic: Even at Gaixia, without the King of Peng the house of Xiang would not have fallen.

sentences.zh: 天下已定，彭王剖符受封，亦欲傳之萬世。

translations.literal: The empire was already settled; the King of Peng split tally and received enfeoffment—also he wished to transmit it ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: Once the realm was settled, the King of Peng received his tally and fief like any other founder, expecting his line to endure forever.

sentences.zh: 今漢壹徵兵於梁，彭王病不行，而疑以為反。

translations.literal: Now Han once called troops from Liang; the King of Peng was ill and did not go, yet was suspected and deemed to have rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Now a single levy from Liang finds him too ill to march, and he is branded a traitor on suspicion alone.

sentences.zh: 反形未見，以苛細誅之，臣恐功臣人人自危也。

translations.literal: Rebellion form not yet seen; by harsh minuteness executing him—your servant fears that merit ministers each will feel danger to himself.
translations.idiomatic: No act of rebellion appeared; to kill him on petty pretexts will make every man who helped you win the throne fear for his life.

sentences.zh: 今彭王已死，臣生不如死，請就亨。」

translations.literal: Now the King of Peng is already dead; your servant to live is not as good as to die; I request to approach the boiling."
translations.idiomatic: Peng Yue is dead; I would rather die with him—send me to the cauldron."

sentences.zh: 上乃釋布，拜為都尉。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon released Bu and appointed him commandant.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor freed him and named him a commandant.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，為燕相，至將軍。

translations.literal: In the time of Filial Wen, he was Yan chancellor, reaching general.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen he rose from chancellor of Yan to general.

sentences.zh: 布稱曰：「窮困不能辱身，非人也；

translations.literal: Bu declared, saying: "In poverty and distress, if one cannot endure humiliation to the body, one is not a man;
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu used to say, "In hardship, if you cannot bear indignity to your person, you are less than human;

sentences.zh: 富貴不能快意，非賢也。」

translations.literal: in wealth and honor, if one cannot satisfy one's will, one is not worthy."
translations.idiomatic: in wealth and rank, if you cannot live as your heart demands, you are no worthy man."

sentences.zh: 於是嘗有德，厚報之；

translations.literal: Thereupon if there had been one who showed him grace, he thickly repaid;
translations.idiomatic: Those who had done him a kindness he repaid lavishly;

sentences.zh: 有怨，必以法滅之。

translations.literal: if there was resentment, he necessarily by law extinguished them.
translations.idiomatic: those who had wronged him he destroyed to the letter of the law.

sentences.zh: 吳楚反時，以功封為鄃侯，復為燕相。

translations.literal: When Wu and Chu rebelled, by merit he was enfeoffed as Marquis of Ju, again was Yan chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: During the rebellion of Wu and Chu he earned the title Marquis of Ju and returned as chancellor of Yan.

sentences.zh: 燕齊之間皆為立社，號曰欒公社。

translations.literal: Between Yan and Qi all for him established shrines, called Luan Duke shrines.
translations.idiomatic: Between Yan and Qi the people founded shrines to him and called them the altars of Duke Luan.

sentences.zh: 布薨，子賁嗣侯，孝武時坐為太常犧牲不如令，國除。

translations.literal: Bu died; his son Ben succeeded the marquis; in the time of Filial Wu, sitting because as Grand Master of Ceremonies the sacrificial victims were not according to ordinance, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: When Luan Bu died, his son Ben inherited the marquisate; under Emperor Wu the fief was stripped because, as grand master of ceremonies, Ben's sacrificial animals failed to meet statute.

sentences.zh: 田叔，趙陘城人也。

translations.literal: Tian Shu was a man of Zhao, Xingcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu was a native of Xingcheng in Zhao.

sentences.zh: 其先，齊田氏也。

translations.literal: His ancestors were the Tian clan of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: His family sprang from the Tian house of Qi.

sentences.zh: 叔好劍，學黃老術於樂鉅公。

translations.literal: Shu loved the sword; he studied Huang-Lao arts with Yue Ju Gong.
translations.idiomatic: He loved fencing and studied Huang-Lao doctrine under Master Yue Ju.

sentences.zh: 為人廉直，喜任俠。

translations.literal: As a man he was incorrupt and straight; he delighted in knight-errantry.
translations.idiomatic: He was scrupulous and blunt-spoken, and delighted in chivalrous deeds.

sentences.zh: 游諸公，趙人舉之趙相趙午，言之趙王張敖，以為郎中。

translations.literal: He roamed among the lords; men of Zhao raised him to Zhao chancellor Zhao Wu, who spoke to Zhao King Zhang Ao, and he was made gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: He moved among the great families until men of Zhao recommended him to Chancellor Zhao Wu, who brought him to the notice of King Zhang Ao of Zhao and had him appointed a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 數歲，趙王賢之，未及遷。

translations.literal: Several years; the Zhao king deemed him worthy, but before there was promotion.
translations.idiomatic: For several years the king admired him but had not yet promoted him.

sentences.zh: 會趙午、貫高等謀弒上，事發覺，漢下詔捕趙王及群臣反者。

translations.literal: It happened that Zhao Wu, Guan Gao and others plotted to assassinate the sovereign; the affair was discovered; Han issued an edict to arrest the Zhao king and the various ministers who were rebels.
translations.idiomatic: Then Zhao Wu, Guan Gao, and others plotted regicide; when the plot was uncovered, Han issued warrants for King Zhang of Zhao and every minister implicated.

sentences.zh: 趙有敢隨王，罪三族。

translations.literal: In Zhao, whoever dared follow the king, crime of three clans.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhao, anyone who dared follow the king would suffer extermination of three clans.

sentences.zh: 唯田叔、孟舒等十餘人赭衣自髡鉗，隨王至長安。

translations.literal: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and more than ten others in crimson garb shaved their own heads and put on the cangue, following the king to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and a dozen others shaved their heads, donned the convict collar and crimson robes, and followed their king to Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 趙王敖事白，得出，廢王為宣平侯，乃進言叔等十人。

translations.literal: The Zhao King Ao's affair became clear; he was able to emerge; the king was abolished and made Marquis of Xuanping; thereupon he advanced speech recommending Shu and the ten men.
translations.idiomatic: When the case against King Zhang Ao was cleared, he was released, demoted from kingship to Marquis of Xuanping, and then recommended Tian Shu and the other nine men to the throne.

sentences.zh: 上召見，與語，漢廷臣無能出其右者。

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned and saw them, conversed with them; no Han court minister could go out to their right.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor received them in audience and, speaking with each in turn, found that none of his ministers could match their caliber.

sentences.zh: 上說，盡拜為郡守、諸侯相。

translations.literal: The sovereign was pleased; all were appointed commandery governors or feudal kingdom chancellors.
translations.idiomatic: Delighted, he appointed them all as governors of commanderies or chancellors to the feudal kings.

sentences.zh: 叔為漢中守十餘年。

translations.literal: Shu was Hanzhong governor for more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu governed Hanzhong for more than a decade.

sentences.zh: 孝文帝初立，召叔問曰：「公知天下長者乎？」

translations.literal: When Emperor Wen first was established, he summoned Shu and asked: "Sir, do you know the elders of the empire?"
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Wen's reign he summoned Tian Shu and asked, "Sir, whom do you count among the most honorable men in the realm?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣何足以知之！」

translations.literal: He replied: "Your servant how is sufficient to know them!"
translations.idiomatic: He answered, "Your servant is far too slight a man to presume to judge that."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「公長者，宜知之。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: "Sir is an elder; you ought to know."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "You are yourself a man of substance; you ought to know."

sentences.zh: 叔頓首曰：「故雲中守孟舒，長者也。」

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said: "The former Yunzhong governor Meng Shu is an elder."
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and said, "The former governor of Yunzhong, Meng Shu, is such a man."

sentences.zh: 是時孟舒坐虜大入雲中免。

translations.literal: At this time Meng Shu sat because the barbarians greatly entered Yunzhong and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Meng Shu had just been removed from office for allowing the Xiongnu to overrun Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「先帝置孟舒雲中十餘年矣，虜常一入，孟舒不能堅守，無故士卒戰死者數百人。

translations.literal: The sovereign said: "The late emperor placed Meng Shu at Yunzhong more than ten years already; the barbarians once entered in force, Meng Shu could not firmly defend; without cause several hundred soldiers who died in battle.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "The late emperor kept Meng Shu at Yunzhong for over ten years; when the barbarians broke in, he failed to hold the line, and hundreds of soldiers died in battle for nothing.

sentences.zh: 長者固殺人乎？」

translations.literal: So elders indeed kill people?"
translations.idiomatic: Is that what we call a man of honor—to let hundreds perish?"

sentences.zh: 叔叩頭曰：「夫貫高等謀反，天子下明詔，趙有敢隨張王者罪三族，然孟舒自髡鉗，隨張王，以身死之，豈自知為雲中守哉！

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said: "As for Guan Gao and others plotting rebellion, the Son of Heaven issued a clear edict that in Zhao whoever dared follow King Zhang would be guilty of the three clans—yet Meng Shu shaved himself and put on the cangue, followed King Zhang, with his body to die for him—how could he have known he would become Yunzhong governor!
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and said, "When Guan Gao and his fellows plotted treason, the Son of Heaven issued an edict that anyone in Zhao who followed King Zhang would suffer extermination of three generations—yet Meng Shu shaved his head, clamped on the collar, and followed the king to share his fate, with no thought that he would ever govern Yunzhong!

sentences.zh: 漢與楚相距，士卒罷敝，而匈奴冒頓新服北夷，來為邊寇，孟舒知士卒罷敝，不忍出言，士爭臨城死敵，如子為父，以故死者數百人，孟舒豈敺之哉！

translations.literal: Han and Chu opposed each other; soldiers were exhausted, and the Xiongnu Modu newly had subjugated the northern tribes and came as border raiders; Meng Shu knew the soldiers were exhausted, could not bear to speak out; knights vied to ascend the walls and die against the foe like sons for a father; for this reason several hundred died—how could Meng Shu have driven them!
translations.idiomatic: Han and Chu were locked in war, the troops were spent, and Modu had just brought the northern tribes to heel and turned them against our borders. Meng Shu saw how worn his men were and could not bring himself to order them to fight; they climbed the walls and died like sons defending a father, and hundreds fell—not because Meng Shu drove them to it!

sentences.zh: 是乃孟舒所以為長者。」

translations.literal: This is the reason Meng Shu is what makes an elder."
translations.idiomatic: That is what makes Meng Shu a true man of honor."

sentences.zh: 於是上曰：「賢哉孟舒！」

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign said: "Worthy indeed is Meng Shu!"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor exclaimed, "Meng Shu is a worthy man indeed!"

sentences.zh: 復召以為雲中守。

translations.literal: Again he was summoned and made Yunzhong governor.
translations.idiomatic: He was summoned back and reappointed governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 後數歲，叔坐法失官。

translations.literal: After several years, Shu by law sat and lost office.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later Tian Shu lost his post for a legal infraction.

sentences.zh: 梁孝王使人殺漢議臣爰盎，景帝召叔案梁，具得其事。

translations.literal: King Xiao of Liang had someone kill Han deliberation minister Yuan Ang; Emperor Jing summoned Shu to investigate Liang and fully obtained the affair.
translations.idiomatic: When King Xiao of Liang had the Han minister Yuan Ang murdered, Emperor Jing sent Tian Shu to investigate the kingdom of Liang, and he uncovered the whole plot.

sentences.zh: 還報，上曰：「梁有之乎？」

translations.literal: Returning he reported; the sovereign said: "Did Liang have it?"
translations.idiomatic: On his return the emperor asked, "Did Liang really do this?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「有之。」

translations.literal: He replied: "There was it."
translations.idiomatic: He answered, "It did."

sentences.zh: 「事安在？」

translations.literal: "Where does the matter stand?"
translations.idiomatic: "Where is the evidence?"

sentences.zh: 叔曰：「上無以梁事為問也。

translations.literal: Shu said: "Your Majesty, do not take Liang's affair as the question.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu said, "Your Majesty should not press me about the affair in Liang.

sentences.zh: 今梁王不伏誅，是廢漢法也；

translations.literal: Now if the Liang king does not submit to execution, this is abolishing Han's law;
translations.idiomatic: If the king of Liang goes unpunished, the laws of Han are void;

sentences.zh: 如其伏誅，太后食不甘味，臥不安席，此憂在陛下。」

translations.literal: if he submits to execution, the empress dowager will not taste sweet in eating, will not be secure on the mat in lying—this worry is on Your Majesty."
translations.idiomatic: if he is executed, the empress dowager will lose her appetite and her sleep, and Your Majesty will bear that grief."

sentences.zh: 於是上大賢之，以為魯相。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign greatly deemed him worthy and made him Lu chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor admired him greatly and appointed him chancellor to the king of Lu.

sentences.zh: 相初至官，民以王取其財物自言者百餘人。

translations.literal: When the chancellor first arrived at office, common people who because the king took their goods came to declare themselves were more than a hundred.
translations.idiomatic: As soon as he took office, more than a hundred commoners petitioned that the king had seized their property.

sentences.zh: 叔取其渠率二千人笞，怒之曰：「王非汝主邪？

translations.literal: Shu took their channel-leaders two thousand men and flogged them, angrily saying: "Is the king not your lord?
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu seized some two thousand ringleaders from the crowd and had them flogged, shouting, "Is the king not your sovereign?

sentences.zh: 何敢自言主！」

translations.literal: How dare you declare yourselves against the lord!"
translations.idiomatic: How dare you bring accusations against your own sovereign!"

sentences.zh: 魯王聞之，大慚，發中府錢，使相償之。

translations.literal: The Lu king heard it, greatly ashamed, issued central treasury money, had the chancellor repay them.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Lu was deeply shamed; he drew funds from the inner treasury and told the chancellor to pay the claimants.

sentences.zh: 相曰：「王自使人償之，不爾，是王為惡而相為善也。」

translations.literal: The chancellor said: "Let the king himself send people to repay them; if not, this is the king doing evil and the chancellor doing good."
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor replied, "Let Your Highness send your own men to make restitution; otherwise it will look as though the king did wrong and the chancellor played the hero."

sentences.zh: 魯王好獵，相常從入苑中，王輒休相就館。

translations.literal: The Lu king loved hunting; the chancellor often followed into the park; the king always rested the chancellor to go to the guest house.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Lu loved the chase; the chancellor always rode with him into the hunting park, but the king would dismiss him to the lodge.

sentences.zh: 相常暴坐苑外，終不休，曰：「吾王暴露，獨何為舍？」

translations.literal: The chancellor often sat exposed outside the park, to the end not resting, saying: "My king is exposed to the elements—how alone should I take shelter?"
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu would sit in the open outside the enclosure and refuse to leave, saying, "If my king endures wind and weather, why should I alone seek shelter?"

sentences.zh: 王以故不大出遊。

translations.literal: The king for this reason not greatly went out to tour.
translations.idiomatic: For that reason the king rarely went out on pleasure hunts.

sentences.zh: 數年以官卒，魯以百金祠，少子仁不受，曰：「義不傷先人名。」

translations.literal: After several years he died in office; Lu used hundred gold for sacrifice; youngest son Ren did not accept, saying: "By righteousness I will not injure the former man's name."
translations.idiomatic: He died in office some years later; Lu offered a hundred catties of gold for his memorial, but his youngest son Tian Ren refused it, saying, "Duty forbids me to trade on my father's good name."

sentences.zh: 仁以壯勇為衛將軍舍人，數從擊匈奴。

translations.literal: Ren by stalwart bravery became guard general retainer, several times followed to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Ren won a place as a retainer to General Wei Qing through his courage and rode with him on repeated campaigns against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 衛將軍進言仁為郎中，至二千石、丞相長史，失官。

translations.literal: Guard general advanced speech making Ren gentleman of the palace, reaching two-thousand-dan, chancellor chief clerk, lost office.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing recommended him until he rose to gentleman of the palace, then to two thousand shi and chief clerk to the chancellor, after which he lost his post.

sentences.zh: 後使刺三河，還，奏事稱意，拜為京輔都尉。

translations.literal: Later he was sent to investigate the Three Rivers; returning, reporting affairs matched the sovereign's intent; he was appointed capital region commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He was later dispatched to inspect the Three Rivers region; his report pleased the emperor, and he was named commandant of the capital area.

sentences.zh: 月餘，遷司直。

translations.literal: After more than a month, he was promoted to director incorrupt.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a month later he was promoted to director of integrity.

sentences.zh: 數歲，戾太子舉兵，仁部閉城門，令太子得亡，坐縱反者族。

translations.literal: After several years, Crown Prince Li raised troops; Ren was in charge of the city gates and closed them in such a way that the crown prince was able to flee; he was convicted for letting the rebel go—clan extermination.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later, when Crown Prince Ju rose in arms, Tian Ren held command of the gates and manipulated their closure so that the heir could escape; for that leniency toward a rebel his entire clan was put to death.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：以項羽之氣，而季布以勇顯名楚，身履軍搴旗者數矣，可謂壯士。

translations.literal: The summation says: In the atmosphere of Xiang Yu's power, Ji Bu by bravery displayed his name in Chu; personally he trod armies and plucked enemy banners several times—he may be called a stalwart.
translations.idiomatic: The historian concludes: In an age ruled by Xiang Yu's tempest, Ji Bu made a name in Chu for sheer nerve; again and again he fought through the ranks and tore down enemy standards—a man in the true mold of the warrior.

sentences.zh: 及至困厄奴僇，苟活而不變，何也？

translations.literal: When it came to distress and slave humiliation, clinging to life without changing—why?
translations.idiomatic: Yet when reduced to chains and public shame, they clung to a humiliating life without yielding their purpose—why?

sentences.zh: 彼自負其材，受辱不羞，欲有所用其未足也，故終為漢名將。

translations.literal: They bore themselves on their talent, received shame without shame, wished to use what was not yet sufficient; therefore in the end became Han famous generals.
translations.idiomatic: They trusted their own abilities; they bore disgrace without flinching because they knew they still had more to give, and so they rose at last to become celebrated commanders of Han.

sentences.zh: 賢者誠重其死。

translations.literal: Worthy men truly weigh their deaths.
translations.idiomatic: Men of true worth do not throw their lives away lightly.

sentences.zh: 夫婢妾賤人，感概而自殺，非能勇也，其畫無俚之至耳。

translations.literal: As for maids, concubines, and other base folk who, stirred by indignation, kill themselves—that cannot be called courage; their reasoning is witless in the extreme.
translations.idiomatic: When bondmaids and concubines slash their wrists in a surge of temper, that is not courage; their thinking is as empty as it can be.

sentences.zh: 欒布哭彭越，田叔隨張敖，赴死如歸，彼誠知所處，雖古烈士，何以加哉！

translations.literal: Luan Bu wept for Peng Yue; Tian Shu followed Zhang Ao; they went to death like returning—they truly knew where they stood; though ancient stalwart martyrs, what could be added!
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu mourned Peng Yue at the risk of boiling oil; Tian Shu followed Zhang Ao in fetters as calmly as if he were going home—they knew exactly what they were about. Even the storied martyrs of antiquity could scarcely surpass them.
